First thing, chunk of content that I just taped the other day, I consider it to be bonus content for the first module of straight line certification. So for those of you have purchased straight line persuasion, the official product, you've seen this before, but you're still going to watch it now in a more intensive fashion, right? And those of you who don't know about this, I think you're really, really fond of this helpful, right? So at the essence is this idea that in the sales training world, right? They always talk about these, you know, two models of selling in terms of that being an old model of selling. And then there's a new model of selling. And they're very different things. Now, in reality, the concept itself is total fucking nonsense. It's grounded in bullshit and mostly self serving of the self serving variety with people who want to like sort of, you know, put a new twist on the sales process and say, oh, this is the room they charge you for, right? So I want us first to mystify it and then explains you the truth about the whole matter, right? So basically to draw it out, you know, how awesome I am when it comes to my, you know, artistic, you know, quality, right? So number one, okay, the old new model is based on the fact that the old model is the sort of hard charging, you know, grab your throat, close, close, close, ABC, always be closing type of sales person, right? So if we use a pie chart here to explain it, so you have, let's say in the model of the old way, you would have what's called, they call rapport building, rapport called wrap rapport building, and they say you should spend 10% of your time building rapport and getting into rapport. Okay, then they say you should spend, let's see how ugly this gets here, 20% of your time, 20% of your time going through questions, meaning you're qualifying, you're qualifying questions. So you want to find out your client's needs, values, right, whatever it might be, qualifying, right? So you ask questions, they answer to understand A, if someone could afford your product, they need your product and so forth, right? Then you spend 30% of your time, this is actually pretty good by the way, 30% of your time presenting. So in other words, essentially you spend 20% of the time finding out really what your prospect needs, 30% of your time presenting to that need, solving that problem, so to speak, right? And then you spend the vast majority of your time closing the deal, close, close, close, right? That's the old model of selling, okay? And it sort of like what you would see in the movie Glenn Gary, Glenn Ross, Boiler Room, even the Wolf of Wall Street, that's the fictitious label of what was going on there because there's nothing like that, okay? But that's what you would think of, right? On the other side of the equation, you have what's called the new model. And the new model is really known as like the consultative model of selling. And let me explain to you what really consultative selling is about. Consultative selling is for people who are scared to say they're salespeople. I'm not really a salesperson. I just talk to people and fill their needs. It's like a fucking joke, okay? So the whole concept of consultative selling is to trick people into thinking that you're not really selling anybody or anything. You're a consultant. You're just a friend. You're the analyst type. You're not a salesperson. You're not an influencer. And it's this ridiculous notion. But let me just tell you how they break it out. So they sort of do a semantic kind of dance here, okay? So with this new model, let's take the same pie chart here, okay? Now you see my talent come out here with the fucking flop side of pie chart, okay? So here, if you add 10% of your time, we report, they say here you spent 40% of your time, let's find more like here, 40% of your time, what they call building relationships, which is basically the poor buying of name, but they call it building relationships, right? Then they say you spent 30% of your time, 30%, this is really bad by the way, 30% of your time, 30% of your time, okay? Essentially gathering intelligence, right? Gathering intelligence. You spend 20, let's say, let's say, identify needs, let's call it identifying needs. That's the real label, identifying needs. Then you spend 20% of your time offering solutions. And this should be 10%, you say, I told you I saw this, 10% of your time, I promised to graphic my guys creators like a billion times better than this, 10% of your time, okay, confirming and closing. In other words, a consultative salesman doesn't have to close. A consultative salesman doesn't have to answer objections. They just simply spend so much time getting to know people and building trust and they understand their prospects so well, there's simply no need to close. It's the idea that you can front load a sale. So remember in straight line terminology, right? We talked about the front half and the back half, right? Even though they're not really has, we use it as just a in the front half of the sale, okay, represents your taking control of the sale, right? Using that control to gather intelligence and build massive rapport, you have your transition, you then make a, you present your solution, your presentation, your solution, right? And then you ask for the order for the first time. That's the front half of the sale. Then when you get to that point, there's three possibilities. Well, according to consultative selling is really never going to be many objections because they just love you so much. You spend so much time building that relationship you're only 10%. I just got to tell you something. In the real world of selling, if you try this, you'll be fucking broke and destitute and out on the street. It's a complete lie and a myth, all right? And I'm not saying that someone who teaches a consultative type of model can't be effective, but they're just lying to you when they say that you're not closing. It's nonsense, okay? And they probably teach you the straight line disguise anyway, because the only way you can close is by using a straight line pattern. They all come down to that in the end. That's what's really happening. But the point is, if you only spent 10% of your time closing, you'd never close. It's not possible. Let me just tell you flat out. As ridiculous as 10% is, it's just as ridiculous to say that you spend 40% of your time closing. You wonder what percentage of the time I spent closing? 100%. I am closing every moment I'm speaking from the moment my prospect lays eyes on me in person. The moment they hear my voice over the phone, I'm fucking closing. Am I closing them on buying my product at that point? No, not exactly. I'm closing them that I'm a person worth listening to, that I can help them achieve their goals. Now, backing that up a step, what does that mean? Well, I'm closing them on the fact that I'm an expert in my field, that I'm sharp. I'm on the ball. I'm a problem solver. I'm enthusiastic. What I have must be good. I'm closing them on that from the moment I open up my mouth, because I know that if I don't successfully close them on feeling that way about them, guess what? They will take control of the sale. And then things spiral out of control. You cannot take someone down the straight line when they're in control. It becomes the crooked line. It becomes the every sale is different scenario. The basic concept of the straight line system, every sale is the same. What does that really mean? Well, it doesn't mean that everyone says the same words. It doesn't mean that everyone has the same belief systems, the same pain points, the same values, hit you with the same objections. It doesn't mean that. Those will all be different. But what always remains the same is that at the end of the day, there are certain core elements that must line up in your prospects mind, in any prospects mind, before you have any shot of closing them. Now, there are three for sure, three for sure, but there's typically more. Usually there's five, it depends on what the person's action threshold is. So there's a very low action threshold. Well, the reality that number is pretty much already taken, that element has been already resolved. You don't have to do it. It's already been addressed because the person has beliefs that say, you know, I just, I love to buy shit. I make quick decisions. I trust people. So that number is out of the equation. And that pretty much takes pain based out of the equation as well for the most part, because you can handle enough pain at the front that you don't really need to reintroduce pain at the end. So in other words, the five, let's go back. I'm assuming here, since you're on this program, right, that you understand what the five core elements are. Let me list them real quickly. So what these five core elements are is number one, before someone's going to buy from you, before they're going to say yes, they have to love your product. People don't buy things that they think are just okay. People don't buy things that they think suck. They don't buy things that they think will make their lives worse. Those human beings by by our very nature, we always operate. This is a very important point guys, so write this down. If you got a pen and a for you or you're gonna phone, write this down. Human beings at the at our nature, we always operate from a position of positive intent, meaning we make decisions that we believe and the operative word is believe that we intend to improve our lives. When we say yes to buying something is because we've said, yeah, I think this will make my life better, not worse. We don't buy things that will make our lives worth, whether we think will make our lives worse. You get it? You present some with an insurance policy or a new car or a software program and when they're running that through the mental computer, no, I think that thing is going to actually make things more complicated or it's going to put me at Morris to make me feel worse about my future. There's no shot. No fucking shot they'll buy. They will not buy. We don't buy things that we think will make our lives worse. We buy things that we think will make our lives better. And again, the word is think because very often we're wrong. People are wrong. There's a lot of serial bad decision makers out there who make a lot of wrong decisions, but at the time they thought they were right based on positive intent. For example, if someone decides against buying your product in the moment, they were saying, you know, I don't believe this. It's not, I don't think it's going to help me. They could probably be wrong. It actually is an amazing product. It will benefit their lives tremendously, but because of positive intent, they don't buy. I mean, look at another way. No one thinks about it and says, well, let me think about this. So I buy this thing and let me fuck my life up and make me keep me up at night and make me feel like an idiot. Okay, I'll take it. You get it? Like it's just logic guys. Okay, so let's let's start in trying to make pretty fancy coatings or labels. People don't buy shit. They think sucks. They buy things that they think are awesome. It will make their lives better. It'll be fun. That will improve some aspect of life. That's why they say, yeah, I'll take my hard earned money and spend it. I'll exert the energy required to put it together or go pick it up or open the box. So simply money is the stored energy because you work hard. You have excess or store it in the back. Boom. When you expend it, you direct expended energy, so stored energy. We don't buy things that we make all think we make our lives worse. Now, the way that plays out on the certainty scale, so let's say a number of 10 is absolute certainty. We're there absolutely certain and damn sure of it. Best thing in the world. A one means it is the biggest piece of shit. I would never be dead wearing that outfit, driving that car, using that computer. It's fucking a rip off. They give shit customer service. It fucking smells. It's ugly. It wastes whatever it might be those with the sum of their opinion is that it's the biggest piece of garbage nonsense. Get it out of my face. That's a one 10. Oh my God. Best things in slice bread. Positive intent. Five means, well, it's like it said, Scott's my bad. She's in business. I'm on the fence. Will someone buy it a five? Probably not. Almost never. Almost never. And a six maybe if they have a really low action threshold, but usually when it's coming this to a seven, seven and a half, like, yeah, it seems pretty good actually, then if someone has a very low action threshold, meaning their beliefs about buying and making decisions are such they just, you know, they don't have to be fully certain before they say yes. That's sort of your cuddle of a positive intent. But if it's a four or a three, like, I don't really like that thing. I don't have, okay, just think about the stupidity of that. Right. It won't work. So what's happening here is that when you're moving someone down the lane, you're corralling these core elements. The first is what they must love the product, right, because of positive intent or else they won't buy. Number two, they must trust and connect with you. And again, positive intent is this because they future pace. Well, if I think by allowing this person into my life, my life's gonna get really crappy. Oh yeah, okay. Yeah, I'll sign up. No one would do it. It always comes down to positive intent at the end of the day. The bitch of it all is that just because someone thinks something is bad doesn't mean it is, or they think it's good doesn't mean it is as well. People can be really bad decision makers. So our job, by the way, is to essentially make sure because positive intent is almost moves in direct lockstep with the certainty scale. How positive someone feels about making a decision is based on how certain they are. The more certain they are, the more you're in the right side of positive intent. And this is what I kind of picked up on, but I this is why I never really spoke about this so much before. It's a very important point to make because it clarifies really what you're doing as a salesperson and why you can't move forward in a sale on the straight line. If someone's not really sure about your product or you or you're coming, you haven't established enough certainty to make sure positive intent doesn't get in the way because everything else could line up. But there's no positive intent. People say, let me think about they don't say, sorry, I'm not sure it makes about like that. They won't say that. They'll just say, let me think about it. And they call you back and they use smoke screens as objections. So these are your first three elements. Trust you, trust accompanying. And there are two more that come behind it. Four is your action threshold. Five is your pain threshold. So these last two don't always come into play. Well, on some level, pain is typically, is, is pain, even a little bit of pain is enough to lubricate the wheels. So whether someone's a buyer in heat or a buyer in power will be related to how much pain they're feeling. There's a buyer in heat. So it's hot. The product, they're feeling some pain, some lack in their life. You get that and that's what makes them a buyer in heat. A buyer in power says, yeah, I need the product, want the product. Can you open that door, please? It's getting really hot in here. Just open it. If you open the door out there too, let's get it drafted. It's just way too hot in here. I'm gonna get the house white. I'm on the beach here, so we don't air condition anyway, but I'm gonna get AC and spokes. It's not possible. We're going to the summer, right? Thank you. Thanks guys. So anyway, you get that. So as you move someone down the line, right? That this idea of is that you're getting past the point of someone's action threshold, meaning the collective level of certainty that someone needs to be at before they're comfortable enough to say yes. Like again, for me, my beliefs about buying, making decisions, how I process fear, my feeling of self-reliance that I can make good decisions quickly, I'm not worried about what others think of me so much, right? That leads to a very low action threshold. So if like, let's say I'm in a 6.5, will I buy? I might buy actually, but if I'm in a 4.5, I will never buy. I'll like, yeah, let me fuck my life up some more. And you understand, positive intent is the fucking magic bullet that drives everything. Now what happens though, is remember for those people who have beliefs that are very much different than mine, they're like, my best decision is a slow decision. I don't trust salespeople. I like to think things through. Those are all beliefs that fall into high action threshold category. Someone who possesses those beliefs is going to have to be absolutely certain about all three 10s and damn sure of it. Otherwise, you know what they'll say? They'll say, hey, let me think about it. Have to be. So again, this idea of what I say every sales the same. Well, number one, those three things always come with these, firstly always come into play. Any one of them is missing. You have negative positive intent and you fuck. It's a monkey wrench and it stops the press from saying yes, that's one side of the equation. Then for those people who have inherently high action thresholds, right, where they like to think things through, they don't like making quick decisions, they don't trust salespeople, those people, you're going to have to attack that fourth number, that action threshold and lower it, very easy to do. And then if that doesn't work, you could also add on more pain, reestablish their pain, they can step into it, talk about it, expand on it, elevate it. And by just the way it works, by definition, the way pain threshold works is an inverse relationship with action threshold. As you add on pain, the action threshold drops. I always tell a story about my dad, this call, love this car, no one could touch his car, but fucking Jimmy at the Sonoco station, right? Well, one day we had 200 miles from home, car breaks down, so the late night is worried, his family's in danger, what does he do? Goes to the first gas station. Why? Pain, fear, worry, pain, fear, worry, those sorts of emotions they will essentially collapse someone's action threshold in the moment. So my dad, yeah, no one touches my car, but Jimmy, unless I'm 200 miles from home and I'm worried sick of my cars broken down and I think these people are going to come and rape my family. Well, then he'll go to the first mechanic he can find. That's the reality. Now it's a very extreme situation, right? But there's so many things we could do through language patterns, there'll be awful things through scarcity that create a very similar response, reaction without having to go that far. And once you add on that pain, so once you add by adding on pain, it lowers the action threshold. Here's another way of looking at it. Imagine a scale, right? We say they have a longer stick here, is there a stick I mean, there's my tennis racket. I'll just use this, right? Let's say this is a look, make this a longer thing, right? And the scales got a fulcrum in the middle. My scale's got a fulcrum, right? This is the fulcrum. So the scales balanced, right? All right, this is your fulcrum in the middle. Now what happens is if the fulcrum is in the middle, right, and you add a weight to this, one pound weight here and a one pound weight there, what happens? They're even. But if the fulcrum is over here, let's say the fulcrum is on the right side, okay, you have one pound and one pound, what happens? So these are your negatives, these are your positives here, your negatives here. If you have a high action threshold, it's like having the fulcrum all the way on this side of the scale. Each positive doesn't give that much weight to things because the fulcrum is over here. On the other side, one little negative steps to scale, they don't buy from you. When you add on pain, what it does is it brings the fulcrum back into the center and it goes, they buy. That's what happens. The same thing goes even when you're just lowering the action threshold using that language pattern, what's the worst that can possibly have? Essentially by taking, this is your fulcrum here, right? And your fulcrum is over here and then you say, what's the worst or not? That much your upsides is massive, your downsides are really, you make them run different moves, it brings the fulcrum back in the middle and people buy. So when I say every sale is the same, what I'm meaning here is that those five elements must line up. At least three and if the first three are not enough, then you've got to raise up their pain threshold and lower their action threshold, all five elements, right? Now, the only possible way that you can do this effectively is if you're in control of the sale. If you're not in control of the conversation, you're screwed. Then every sale truly is different because you cannot go about lining those five things up in a strategic way. You see, I know when I walk into an encounter, I know exactly the order in which I'm going to essentially line up those five elements. I know exactly, because I'm doing it the same way every time. My prospects might say different things to me. Do they have different values, different pain points, different objections, but those five elements, as I overcome all that other stuff, those five elements are always being lined up in the same order and using the same strategies because he perceives me as an expert or she perceives me as an expert, as a figure of authority, as a force to be reckoned with. So what do they do? They say, okay, you're an expert, show me how. Okay, fair enough. Go ahead, expert, show me how to do it. And they listen. And then I say, okay, well, first of all, then I start asking questions, and I slowly take them down the line. If you're not taking control of the sale, you're screwed. So look at it this way. In the first four seconds, I am closing my prospect that I am a person worth, I'm closing them on that. When I ask to gather intelligence, there's certain things I do. I ask permission to ask questions. I ask big picture questions first. I ask noninvasive questions first. Why? Because I'm closing them on feeling comfortable and it's worth their time out of their busy day and also worth the fear of being embarrassed or found out, disclosing personal info to someone they really don't know. It's worth it. I'm closing them on that. And when I say to them, well, Jim, Jill, based on what you said to me, this is a perfect fit for you. I'm closing him that damn, I'm right, I'm closing them the next step. On some level, sales and marketing is always closing when I'm taking the next step. You're always closing them on taking the next step. So this nonsense, let's go back now, you get this. This is what you really get this. I'm telling you, sales starts to become very easy because all the other stuff like tonality, bilage, I lay it out for you, it's still a platter. But if the context of what a sale really means is not clear to you, then you'll twist in the wind. If you don't understand what positive intent is about, you don't understand what you're really doing when you're talking, what you're doing is you're constantly closing them 100% of the time on taking that next step in the process, whatever that step might be. In addition, moving on to rapport, the idea that rapport is in some way something that only has to be managed or built in a defined portion of the sale. Well, in the case of this, we have 10% and here we have 40%. That's fucking nonsense. By the way, the second you break rapport, at any moment, time you fall out of rapport, guess what? You can't influence and they will never buy from you. I am constantly building rapport. I have, and here's, let me give you an even better one. I am constantly on watch. I'm standing constant visual with my eyes and my ears looking for a cue, listening for a cue that my prospect has fallen even slightly out of rapport with me. Yeah, I'm fucking vigilant about it and if I sense that, I will slow down, I will back up, I'll do whatever I have to do to go back and build, get back in and build on that because if at any moment in time you're out, you can't influence, you're done. So how the fuck could it be 10% or 40%? It doesn't make sense. It's not the real world of influence. That's why it doesn't work. Next, presenting. One way you present 30%, the other 20%. It's not, it's not technical. People always say, let me think about it. People don't lay down guys and as sooner you fucking realize this, the more wonderful your life will be in sales. It's okay. But the notion that you could just be so tightly in rapport with someone and have spent so much time identifying what really matters, identifying their needs, right? That they're just like, fuck, just tell me what you got. Tell me what you got in three quick sentences so I can buy it from you. Or the fact that in my presentation, that's the point that I need to make sure I really script that part out. See, when I'm presenting, that's my formal solution. So I've got to prepare for that. Make sure that's exactly right. The rest of the stuff I can kind of wing. Good fucking luck trying to close like that. There's no free words, no type of stupid statements, no time to go off the Pluto, or talk about the price of tea in China, with a straight line system, every word is powerful and it's meaning. It's directed. And that directive and that meaning is to keep them on the line moving towards the close. Or else you're in Pluto. It doesn't work. My presentation starts from the first word out of my mouth. Yeah. If anything, I spend the most time on the first 30 seconds. I will spend more time on that than probably anything in my script. And when I say script, if you're in person, I still have a script I memorized. I'm going to say I spend more time on that first 30 seconds of positioning because I know that I'm being judged and they're hanging on my every word. If it doesn't really make sense to them at a high level, I can't take control. And then I also know that as important as those first five seconds are and the follow up 10 seconds to make sure that you're right, you can never stop being vigilant about what words are because if you stay stupid shit, they will realize you're not an expert. You're not the real deal and they'll break the point. You understand this nonsense that there's a partition with what you're doing. It's just not true. You're always doing all things at once. And by the way, without that, you know what happens? You break all the rules of influence and you don't close. That's what happens when you try to segment stuff out into boxes. You inadvertently break rule after rule of influence and you're closed at the low. You know what you're closed? People with really low actions. There's this would buy from anyone. You don't close the ones who are tougher and that's what real salesmen do and that's why they get paid so much top producers close the ones that others feel are difficult. They know how to do it and they are always aware that every word out of their mouth is being judged analyzed hang on. There are no free words. And then getting back to the end of it. The actual closing. It's just nonsensical. As a salesperson, you're doing all things at once and that brings us back to this whole notion of ABC always be closing. What people mistake about this, they think that ABC is about that you're always closing. You're always trying to get to buy from you. No, it's not what it means. ABC means that you're always closing them on whatever that step of the process is at that moment because as soon as you stop doing that, you're done. Okay. Remember these two models are both nonsense because you're always doing all things at once. And rather than it being about what am I building before it's about following the pathway to line up the core elements of influence. And we know if we do that people will buy from us. That's the important point here. Okay. Next, I want to switch that now to a completely different topic. Okay. I want to talk about something on the inner game which has been coming up a lot lately. Okay. I did a podcast yesterday. Yesterday or two days ago. And I won't mention with you because we're one of the biggest podcasters in the world, one of the biggest podcasters. Right? And you guys know I'm launching my own podcast by the way. Next two weeks or three weeks, it'll be out. It's going to be awesome. It's called the Wolves Den. You definitely want to subscribe. I'm going to show you all of them. It's going to be hysterical. It's going to be a snowflake fucking free zone. There's no safe spaces here. The fucking wolf will be on the attack bearing fangs. Anyway, point being, I did this podcast and one of the things that really came up was belief systems about beliefs. I think it was maybe a year ago. I'm sure very few of you have seen me do this. I went through this chart that I referred to as the cycle of beliefs. How beliefs get formed and how they end up tripping you up. So let me just back up and explain what a belief really is. A belief is essentially, it's something that you think is true. It's something that you think to be true. I think I'm great at tennis. I believe I'm great at tennis. I believe in the world. I think it's true. It's not a fact. Beliefs are not facts of life. Beliefs are the things about the world, about ourselves, that we think to be true. You have something called identity beliefs. I'm too old. I'm too young. I'm too fat. I'm too thin. I'm handsome. I'm ugly. I'm gregarious and outgoing. I'm a loner. I'm introvert. Beliefs, there's all fucking beliefs. There's nothing empirically right or wrong. They're belief systems. Now, on the flip side, the question is, well, what causes beliefs to be formed? Well, at the end of the day, what really forms beliefs are these root experiences that we have that serve as almost like it starts with a root experience. And then once something happens to us, that experience changes the way we perceive things. And we start actually cutting out things that don't agree with this belief. And we narrow and narrow our field of focus. And we only see things and notice things that line up with our beliefs and we delete things that don't. That's how it works. Now, I'm going to get back to that in a second. Let me just give you the other type of beliefs. So the other type, one was identity. The second is capability beliefs. What you're capable of. I am capable of being, getting rich. I'm capable of making my own way in life. I'm capable of closing anyone who's closable. I'm capable of massive compassion. I'm capable of being a great public speaker. If you don't think you'd be a great public speaker, I got news. So you know what? You ain't going to want to do public speaking. If you don't think you can close, you're not going to try to close. But you don't try things that we think we're not good at. We don't try. We don't go. We don't go for the brass ring. If we don't believe we're going to get that brass ring, you know why it makes sense. Because again, it's almost positive intent. The brain says, what's the point? Why do all the work? Why should I go and spend a hundred or a thousand man hours and when I really know my heart of hearts, I don't really have what it takes. I'm not really that sort of guy or girl. I don't really possess all the requisite skills. I don't really see myself succeeding. It's the capability belief. Now, let me show you this chart of beliefs here and how it works. So I'll try my hand at being in this world class honest again. And he says, by the way, there's a reason I'm such a terrible artist. So watch, here's the way it works. You have four boxes. And essentially, this first box is what we have is called pure potential. Meaning it's like we're born on day one and we're perfect. Now, I understand some people are got get belted very bad debt and they physical limitations really. And there are those people, this does not refer to that. So if you God, God, I hope you're not that person, but if you have mentally challenged in some way, or you're physically challenged and you want to become a basketball player, you get this reality here too. But when I'm talking about the general way we move through the world, on day one, we have generally pure potential. That's day one. They were born. Then comes day two of our lives, right? After our parents start to fucking take it away. Because here's what happens. You start with pure potential. And then as you start moving through life, you take actions. Okay. Okay. And based on those actions right here, you take actions, right? And based on those actions, you are going to get what you're going to get results, both positive and negative. And then based on those results, you're going to form a belief about who you are and what your cable of friends. Let's say you go out and you're in third grade and the teacher says to you, okay, I want you to come Jimmy, time for you to give your speech in front of the class now. And Jimmy stands up. He's nervous because no one really taught Jimmy how to do public speaking. Jimmy didn't prepare himself. He hasn't, he didn't really write out his speech. He doesn't have this coherent thoughts and assertions to make. Jimmy's about to go up and wing it. And he's not one of those people who was, I was like that who could wing it and do great. Jimmy is not a natural born closer or speaker. So what happens? Jimmy's average. So Jimmy walks up in front of the room, his heart's beating a mile a minute. So he's like, what are you supposed to do? And as he starts talking to kids like, and everyone starts making faces and Jimmy starts to feel pain and uncomfortability. Jimmy got a negative result. Got a very poor result. As Jimmy watches the people snicker and laugh at him, he feels pain inside, emotional hurt, right? And he forms a belief saying what? Well, number one, people are mean, but two, I'm just not good at public speaking. I'm not, I'm not, I'm not a good speaker. I'm not good. I hate, and he goes, and then he goes, I hate public speaking. I never want to do this again. And Jimmy starts to develop a fear of public speaking at an actual limitation because what happens is because of this belief and the fear associated, what happens as he goes back to do it again, his potential was diminished. Because next time, fresh with the memory of the disaster of last time, what happens? Well, people typically do not, it's very rare that some will actually practice something they suck at. And unless they're really being pushed by someone, in which case, it's a very good thing. But what happens typically is the next time Jimmy says, I'm not good at that. I don't want to do that again. He won't prepare. He won't study. He won't try to overcome it. All right. And next time he comes up, he's paralyzed even more. So what happens to his, his, takes the action again, his results even worse. What happens? His belief is reinforced. I am really bad. I suck. I'm the worst. Let me give you a bad example here with me. I'm the, I'm a terrible artist, right? I really am. Right? Let me ask you an honest question. You know, heart to heart, do you think that I'm missing an art gene? Is there something wrong with me? I'm a pretty talented person normally, right? Am I missing the gene that I just simply can't draw a line? I can't draw a bowl of fruit. I can't paint. Is there something wrong with me? Am I deficient in something? Is my DNA fucked up? No. It's not at all. Let me tell you what happened to me. I'll tell you exactly what happened. When I was in kindergarten, I had this teacher named Mrs. Roskin. All right. I just finished going through the tonality module, advanced module, in the straight-line certification, when we actually separate tonality into different models, you have basic tonality and then advanced tonality. So the first part of tonality is like unconscious communication, usually the background, the understanding, the basis, the fabric, and then the second time we go through them, we have a couple other learnings, let me go back to it and then we get specific and have a ploy at what they are, right? One of the interesting things that came up is that, what really happens with tonality, people, and I didn't realize this until I started training salesmen, is that when I was out there in the early days selling myself, I had just unbelievable tonality, it's natural, it's naturally great with tonality, perfect tonality, perfect right on, right? I began to notice though that a lot of people had awful tonality, they had terrible tonality, they didn't like monotone, they just couldn't get it, so when I started to train salesmen, I'd say, okay guys, I say like, okay, name of the company, and I'd say, now you say that, and they'd say name of the company, I'm like, no, no, no, no, you don't, I'm like, no, I want you to sound absolutely certain, the guy said, name of the company, I'm like, wait, dude, that's not certain, that's not certain, I want you to sound, I want you to say name of the company, arrow times making up words here, arrow times, international cutting edge, I take from, I want you to say name of the company, something wrong with their ear, you understand, there's a flaw, they think they sound a certain way and they don't, and even when I press them and I press them and I press them to keep, you don't sound like they won't do it, what I ultimately figured out was a shortcut to help people who are really struggling with tonality, and that shortcut is using your body physiology to help you elicit certain tonalities, for instance, there was a girl who I was training on tonality on stage, and I said, I want you to lower your voice into a whisper, I want you to create scarcity here, and I kept saying, now Jim, the reason for the call today, something just came across my desk, and she'd be, Jim, the reason for the call today, I'm like, no, no, lower your voice, Jim, the reason, she couldn't fucking do it. Now, I know that might seem outlandish, but it might be you, because you wouldn't realize it if it were you, people struggle with this shit, and it rips them apart and suddenly goes, tonality's everything, right? You know what I did to fix it? I said, I want you to do this, and I think it was Cindy, Cindy, I want you to narrow your eyes, it's just a slits, okay, and crinkle up your nose, and I want you to say the same words, she goes, well, Jim, sure enough, she lowers her voice into a whisper, she goes, well, reason, you can use your body to manifest certain tonalities, it's unbelievable. Watch when I say Jim, certainty, I say Jim, all I'm asking for, you'll see me do this all the time, others, you wish it, sound fair enough? Edgar says, Q, oh, well he's asking me, but what are four sides in context of boxing someone in, when exactly do you, no, this is, he's just asking me to give to you. Okay, good. All right. What are the four sides in context of boxing someone in, when exactly do you know when you have blocked every exit ramp? So, so boxing someone is, is a term I don't really use much anymore because it has a negative connotation. It wasn't meant to be that way, but you know, you know, over time, you just, you know, learn to say things more elegantly, right? You know, boxing someone in what it really means is that you're using consistency at a very high level. The consistency is one of Robert Cheldini's core influencing, you know, it's a linchpin for influence. Consistency, scarcity, reciprocity, authority, likability, social proof, those are Cheldini's influences, right? Consistency means that people want to remain consistent with things they've said out loud and things they've done. So when someone says, oh, I really love this, then you have a much better shot of them saying, I'm not gonna buy it. First, someone saying, I hate that, I think this thing sucks. Then once they say that, it's like digging the heels in the sand. The lowest level of consistency is, you know, when you say to someone, get them into the habit of saying yes, by somehow doing that, then when you ask for the order, they'll say yes. That's just not true, but you certainly wouldn't want to get so into the habit of saying no, but that's a very low level of it, right? So in terms of consistency, it's a very powerful thing. Boxing someone in is when you say to someone, well, Jim, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And they say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. That's not a 10. And then I say, oh, exactly. It really is a great idea. In fact, let me say this. I loop back, I give a second pattern. And now after the second pattern, and I say, you see what I'm saying, Jim? You're like, oh, no, yeah, it's great. Oh my God, yeah. I say exactly. And then as I keep moving down the line, I'll start doing the same thing for the second 10, the third 10. And each time they keep saying, no, I'm looping back, creating more certainty and more certainty. What's happening is as I keep getting to say, yeah, if I knew you, yes. If I knew you, I'd be doing it right now. I love the product. I love the product. And I could see myself in the future feeling great, doing a box, besides the box. I love the prology. Feel myself in the future, future is more, that's a box, right? And then they say, but I don't trust you. Then you cut that out. Let me tell you more about myself. And you say, and then you make them trust you at your forest gun pattern, and then trust the company on the bottom. It's large. It's that it's what you're doing is, is that you're getting people say, yeah, I, if I knew you, yes, I understand the product is awesome. I don't know you well enough. I say, I get that the box is there. Now let me tell you about myself. And when they're done, they're like, Oh, and then you get to say, Oh, my, it sounds all they start to become, they almost have to a certain point. You said, you know, Jim, let's say I'm wrong. Okay. And you have to, and you, and the product doesn't work as well. You cash in your guarantee. You have zero loss. He'll say, yeah, on the upside though, if it's even half, right, you saw the future pace, it gets to a point where there's no really reasonable reason to say, reasonable reason. There's no reason that's reasonable for them to say, let me think. You're taking away their, they don't have to go against their own belief systems, their own prior statements, their own anchors, what they think is proper decision making and normal human interactions, because of doing the right thing by others. And you're learning every possibility why someone could rationally say no and feel good about it. That's boxing someone in, but I don't want you to do that in an unethical. It's not unethical. This is just sales. That's what you do when you sell. When you sell, you're just making this such a great case to them and eliminating these bullshit sort of excuses that people have. They're just smoke screens. They're not really thinking through and you, and you bring it to the forefront and you get them. So you know what? I really just, I just don't, I don't really have, that's exactly what happened with the guy on the, go back to the dairy sale. It's a perfect example of boxing someone in. It's a straight line. It's a simple thing. It was bad. You'd flag the first one. You lose. You build certainty about the product, yourself, the company. I hear him doing this all the time. If I walk by him, he's running a pattern or some looping pattern. He's always doing it all day. Seriously, he's done it all day long. Just remember this. Don't get so caught up in this. Does the idea make sense to you? It doesn't always fit. Like it's not, it's not about that. That's an anchor phrase I used to explain to you. The first thing is the product. Sometimes it'll work. Sometimes not. Just remember what you're doing. Notice you're looping around and around and sometimes you deflect the first objection. But it's not always, well, I hear what you're saying. It's common sometimes. The point is that you're always essentially running this basic, very simple strategy of I deflect the first objection. I find out how certain they are about my product. I then make them a lot more certain. I do a certain to check for myself, the company, ask for the order again. Next, I'll do another loop back. I'll do the same thing and then lower their act. It's really easy, guys. It's probably the easiest part of the straight line, but the hardest to really become perfect. It's easy to do it poorly and it still works when you do it poorly.
